refinement_sections.content: The goal is to develop a syntactic parser capable of analyzing English sentences, identifying and labeling various syntactic structures such as nouns, verbs, phrases, and clauses. The parser should primarily focus on declarative sentences while also accommodating interrogative and imperative forms. The output will be a software prototype that showcases the parser's functionality through a series of sample sentences.

The parser will be designed to handle a range of syntactic structures, but due to practical constraints, it may not cover all dialectal variations or highly complex sentences. The focus will be on standard American English, and the parser's performance will be evaluated based on its ability to accurately parse sentences within this scope.

The design will incorporate contemporary linguistic theories such as X-bar theory and dependency grammar to inform the parsing algorithms. The parser will utilize a combination of rule-based and statistical methods to enhance accuracy and efficiency.

As no specific dataset is provided, a curated set of sample sentences will be created, including a mix of declarative, interrogative, and imperative sentences. These examples will be drawn from various sources, such as literature, news articles, and conversational transcripts, ensuring a diverse representation of syntactic structures.

The parser's performance will be evaluated using precision, recall, and F1-score metrics, aligning with industry standards for natural language processing tasks. Additionally, a qualitative assessment will be conducted through user feedback on the clarity and accuracy of the parsing results.

A comprehensive report will accompany the software prototype, detailing the design choices, assumptions made during development, and the methodologies employed for both parsing and evaluation. This report will also include discussions on potential future improvements and extensions of the parser.
